planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in tropical or subtropical climates (zone 10–12) in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, acidic soil in partial shade; Tongkat Ali is a slow-growing, small to medium understory tree native to the primary and secondary rainforests of Malaysia, Indonesia, Vietnam, and the Philippines, whose long taproots are one of the most commercially important medicinal herbs of Southeast Asia — the long, bitter, orange-red roots are used as a traditional adaptogen and tonic for energy, stress resistance, and male health.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, acidic, forest-type soil in partial shade to deep shade; Eurycoma longifolia requires the warmth, humidity, and consistent moisture of a tropical rainforest environment — avoid cold, drought, or standing water, and provide forest-floor-type organic mulch to mimic its natural habitat. Eurycoma longifolia (Tongkat Ali, Longjack, or Malaysian Ginseng) is a slender, dioecious, understory tree native to the lowland and hill dipterocarp rainforests of Peninsular Malaysia, Sumatra, Borneo, Vietnam, and the Philippines, recognized as the most commercially important and widely exported medicinal plant of Southeast Asia by trade value. The long, slender, deeply penetrating taproots — sometimes over 6 feet long and difficult to harvest — are the medicinal part, dried, powdered, and used in traditional Malay, Indonesian, and Vietnamese medicine as a bitter tonic for fatigue, stress, fever, and debility. Modern phytochemical research has identified quassinoids (eurycomanone) and other bioactive compounds with adaptogenic, testosterone-supporting, anti-fatigue, and antimalarial properties, driving a large international dietary supplement industry. Tongkat Ali is now one of the most over-harvested wild medicinal plants in Southeast Asia — Malaysia has established cultivation standards and sustainable harvesting programs to prevent wild population collapse. Cultivation from seed is slow; plant in deep, organically rich, moist, shaded conditions similar to rainforest understory.
sources: Malaysian Biotechnology Corp — Tongkat Ali (Eurycoma longifolia)

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in tropical or subtropical climates (zone 10–12) in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade with strong climbing support; Cat's Claw is a large, woody tropical liana native to the Amazon and Orinoco rainforests of Peru, Brazil, and Bolivia — one of the most widely sold medicinal herbs of the Americas — whose thorned bark and root contain alkaloids with well-documented anti-inflammatory and immune-modulating properties used across the Amazon Basin for centuries.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade with a large, strong support structure — trellis, pergola, or tree — for the vine to ascend; Uncaria tomentosa is a vigorous grower in tropical conditions and requires annual pruning to manage size and direct growth. Uncaria tomentosa (Cat's Claw or Uña de Gato) is a large, woody liana in the Rubiaceae (coffee) family native to the humid tropical rainforests of the Amazon Basin, particularly Peru, Brazil, Bolivia, Colombia, and Ecuador, where it climbs to the forest canopy using distinctive, recurved, claw-like thorns at the leaf nodes — the feature giving it its common name. The thorned inner bark and root bark are the primary medicinal parts, containing a unique array of pentacyclic oxindole alkaloids (mitraphylline, isomitraphylline, rhynchophylline) with well-documented anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and immune-modulating activities. Cat's Claw has been used for centuries by Peruvian Asháninka and other Amazonian peoples to treat arthritis, gastrointestinal inflammation, infections, and cancer — and is now one of the top-selling botanical supplements in the United States and Europe for arthritis and joint pain. Bark is harvested sustainably by removing sections of outer bark without felling the vine. In the garden, it is an ornamental, fast-growing tropical vine with attractive, paired opposite leaves and small, tubular, white flowers.
sources: University of Maryland Medical Center — Cat's Claw

subcategory: West African shade-loving sweet protein plant

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions or container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, acidic soil in deep shade in tropical or subtropical climates (zone 10–12); Miracle Fruit Vine (Katemfe) is a large, shade-loving West African forest-floor monocot — not a vine despite the common name — whose triangular red fruits contain thaumatin, a natural protein approximately 100,000 times sweeter than sucrose by weight, giving the fruit its 'miracle' designation as the world's sweetest natural substance.

directions: Plant in deep shade in consistently moist, humus-rich, acidic, well-drained soil; Thaumatococcus daniellii is adapted to the humid tropical rainforest floor and suffers in direct sunlight, dry conditions, or cold below 55°F — provide a consistently shaded, humid microenvironment and water regularly to maintain soil moisture. Thaumatococcus daniellii (Miracle Fruit Vine, Katemfe, or Katamfe) is a large, rhizomatous, forest-floor monocot in the Marantaceae family (prayer plant family) native to the humid rainforests of West and Central Africa from Sierra Leone east through Nigeria and Cameroon to the Congo Basin. Its common name 'Miracle Fruit Vine' is somewhat misleading — it is not a vine, but a large, clumping, ground-level herb with broad, long-stalked, oval leaves resembling a giant prayer plant. The small, triangular, red fruits (about 1 inch long) contain thaumatin — a naturally occurring protein recognized as the sweetest natural substance on earth, approximately 2,000–100,000 times sweeter than sucrose on a weight basis — used traditionally in West Africa to sweeten bitter foods and fermented beverages. Thaumatin (E957) is approved as a natural sweetener in the EU and many countries and is commercially extracted for use in food products and chewing gum. The plant requires conditions mimicking deep African rainforest understory — deep shade, high humidity (above 70%), consistent moisture, and warm temperatures year-round; it does not tolerate cold or dry spells. Note: not to be confused with Synsepalum dulcificum (miracle berry), a separate shrub with a different taste-modifying effect.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Thaumatococcus daniellii
FAO Food and Nutrition Paper — Thaumatin

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, fertile to average soil in zone 8–11; Indian Plum (Paniala) is a multipurpose South and Southeast Asian Flacourtia shrub or small tree bearing clusters of small, round to oval, red-to-dark-purple berries with juicy, sweet-tart, plum-like flesh that are eaten fresh, preserved, and made into jams across South Asia — more palatable than the related Governor's Plum (F. indica) when fully ripe.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, fertile to poor soil; Flacourtia jangomas is drought tolerant once established and adaptable to a wide range of soils including lateritic, sandy, and slightly calcareous soils — water regularly only in establishment. Flacourtia jangomas (Indian Plum, Paniala, or Paniyala) is a thorny shrub or small tree native to South Asia from India and Sri Lanka east through Bangladesh, Myanmar, Thailand, and Malesia, cultivated and semi-wild in village gardens and homestead agroforestry systems across the Indian subcontinent and Southeast Asia for its clusters of small (0.5–1 inch), round, red-to-deep-purple, juicy fruits. The fruits are sweeter and less astringent than F. indica when fully ripe — tasting of a mild plum with slight tartness — and are eaten fresh, made into jams, chutneys, pickles, and preserves. Ripe fruits stain dark purple-red and can be fermented into a mild wine. The plant is dioecious (male and female on separate plants); at least one male tree is required among a group of female plants for fruit set. Flacourtia jangomas is very similar in growth habit and care to F. indica and makes a productive, low-maintenance fruiting shrub or edible hedge plant for zone 8–11 gardens in warm, sunny, well-drained conditions.
sources: Morton, J. — Fruits of Warm Climates (1987)

subcategory: Brazilian Caatinga native dryland fruit tree

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in full sun in very well-drained, sandy to stony, poor to average soil in zone 9–11; Imbu (Umbuzeiro) is a remarkable drought-adapted fruit tree endemic to the Brazilian Caatinga semi-arid scrubland — one of the most important wild food trees of the Brazilian Northeast — that stores water in massive, starchy, underground tubers and produces small, round, yellow-green, sweet-sour fruits vital to subsistence food security in one of the world's driest inhabited regions.

directions: Plant in full sun in very well-drained, sandy to stony, poor to average soil; Spondias tuberosa is exceptionally drought tolerant — the underground water-storage tubers allow the tree to survive months of total drought; overwatering or waterlogged soil is fatal. Spondias tuberosa (Imbu, Umbú, or Umbuzeiro) is a drought-deciduous, small to medium tree endemic to the Brazilian Caatinga (semi-arid thorny scrubland) of the northeastern states of Pernambuco, Bahia, Sergipe, Piauí, and Ceará — one of the most important and beloved wild fruit trees of the Brazilian Northeast. The tree's defining ecological adaptation is remarkable: a system of massive, water-filled, carrot-to-sweet-potato-sized underground storage tubers (technically water-storing roots) that can accumulate hundreds of kilograms of water, allowing the tree to survive the brutal, multi-month droughts of the Caatinga and burst into fruit production with the first rains — typically January through March. The small (1–1.5 inch), round, pale-yellow to yellow-green fruits have juicy, sweet-sour, tropical, mango-lychee-like pulp eaten fresh, made into the beloved Brazilian drink umbuzada (fruit pulp boiled with milk and sugar), jams, and fermented into Caatinga wine. The tree has become a symbol of northeastern Brazilian cultural identity and food resilience. Spondias tuberosa is well adapted to dryland gardens in zone 9–11 where excellent drainage and full sun are provided; it is one of the most drought-tolerant fruiting trees available.
sources: Morton, J. — Fruits of Warm Climates (1987)

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in cool, moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial shade in zone 8–10; African Cherry is a slow-growing, large montane forest tree native to the afromontane forests of sub-Saharan Africa, grown for its anti-inflammatory phytosterol-rich bark — one of the most widely prescribed botanical medicines in Europe for benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) — and critically threatened by over-harvesting; sustainable cultivation from nursery-grown trees is the responsible sourcing option.

directions: Plant in cool, moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade or full sun in cooler climates; Prunus africana requires the cool, moist conditions of montane forest environments — it performs poorly in hot, humid tropical lowlands and is best suited to highland subtropical and warm-temperate climates. Prunus africana (African Cherry, Pygeum, or African Plum) is a large, slow-growing, evergreen tree native to the montane forests of sub-Saharan Africa — occurring on nearly every major mountain range from Mt. Cameroon and the Ruwenzori to the Ethiopian Highlands, Drakensberg, and Kilimanjaro above 1,500m elevation — and listed on CITES Appendix II due to severe overharvesting of its medicinal bark. The bark contains a rich complex of phytosterols (beta-sitosterol, beta-sitostenone), ferulic acid esters, and n-docosanol with well-documented anti-inflammatory and anti-proliferative effects on prostate tissue — making standardized Pygeum bark extract one of the most widely prescribed botanical medicines in France, Germany, and Italy for benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) symptoms. The annual global trade in Prunus africana bark exceeds 4,000 metric tons, putting wild populations under severe pressure across its range. Cultivating nursery-grown trees (which require 8–15 years to produce harvestable bark) is a sustainable alternative to wild collection. The small, cherry-like, red-to-black fruits are edible but very bitter; primarily a timber and medicinal tree.
sources: WHO Traditional Medicine — Pygeum africanum bark
